title: How to Rent a Boat for a Bachelorette or Stag Party
topic: Renting a boat for a bachelorette or stag party
html:
A few hours out on the water can turn an ordinary send-off into the highlight of the whole celebration. There is something about a deck, a coastline and a group of close friends that makes the day feel special before anyone has even left the harbour. If you are planning a hen do or a stag weekend and want something more memorable than another round of bar crawls, a boat trip is hard to beat. The good news is that organising one has never been simpler. Below is a practical guide to getting it right, from choosing the right vessel to keeping the group safe and the budget under control.

Why a boat works so well for a send-off

The appeal is partly the setting and partly the privacy. On a boat the group has the space to itself, with no queue at the bar and no strangers crowding the table. You set the music, the pace and the route. For a milestone celebration that exclusivity matters, and it explains why so many planners now rent a boat instead of booking yet another restaurant. A morning of swimming and snorkelling, an afternoon of sunbathing and a sunset cruise back to port can all happen on the same day, with the scenery doing most of the entertaining for you.

Choosing the right boat for your group

The size and style of the vessel should follow the mood you are after. A lively party of twelve has very different needs from a relaxed group of six, so be honest about how you all want to spend the time. As a rough guide:

  • Motor yachts suit groups who want speed, comfortable cabins and the option to reach a quieter cove away from the crowds.
  • Catamarans are stable, roomy and ideal for sunbathing, dancing and larger numbers, which makes them a favourite for hen and stag parties.
  • Sailing yachts appeal to groups who enjoy a slower, more scenic pace and do not mind a little hands-on involvement.
  • Speedboats and RIBs work for short, high-energy outings and smaller groups chasing a quick thrill.

When you compare a boat rental, look closely at the passenger limit, the amount of shaded deck space and whether there is a swimming platform. A clear difference between capacity and comfort matters: a boat licensed for fifteen people will feel cramped if all fifteen want to lie down at once.

With or without a skipper

Most party groups are better off with a crew. A professional skipper handles the navigation, knows the best swimming spots and lets everyone relax without worrying about the conditions. Bareboat options, where you take the helm yourself, are usually only available to those who hold a recognised licence and have logged genuine sea time. If nobody in the group is qualified, choose a crewed trip and treat the skipper as part of the experience. Many will happily suggest a route, recommend a lunch stop ashore and keep the day running smoothly while you simply enjoy it.

Budget, timing and what to ask before you book

Prices vary with the season, the size of the boat and the length of the charter, so book early for peak summer dates when the best vessels go quickly. Before you confirm anything, it helps to browse boats in your destination and shortlist two or three that fit the numbers. When you message an owner, ask the following:

  1. What exactly is included: fuel, the skipper, water toys, drinks or any cleaning fee at the end.
  2. The deposit, the cancellation terms and what happens if the weather turns.
  3. Whether you can bring your own food and drink, or whether catering is arranged on board.
  4. The pick-up point, the start time and how long the trip actually runs on the water.

Getting these answers in writing avoids awkward surprises on the day. If you would rather speak to the owner directly, a quick conversation often clears up the small details a listing cannot cover, and most are quick to reply once you decide to hire a boat for the occasion.

Keeping the day safe and considerate

A celebration on the water is still a day at sea, so a few sensible habits go a long way. Pace the drinks, keep water and sunscreen flowing, and make sure everyone knows where the life jackets are stored. Decorations are welcome, but skip anything that scatters litter into the sea, such as confetti or single-use plastics. Treat the boat with the same care you would want for your own, and tip a hard-working crew if the service was good.

The best send-offs feel effortless because the planning was done in advance. Sort the boat, the route and the essentials early, then let the day take care of itself.

Ready to set sail

A boat party gives a hen or stag celebration a sense of occasion that a venue on land rarely matches. Pick the right vessel, ask the right questions and the rest tends to fall into place. Compare options and secure a yacht charter for your group, and if you happen to own a boat yourself, you can just as easily list your boat and earn from it between trips.
